Nimble, compact and relatively obedient, although not always on their best behaviour.
Delivery robots like these have become increasingly common on UK streets over the past decade.
From Leeds to Milton Keynes, the machines bring online orders of food and goods right
to your door at top speeds of four miles per hour.
Companies behind the technology say robots can be faster, safer, and better for the environment than deliveries by road.
But for some locals living in the trial areas, they can be a bit of a nuisance.
I find them quite menacing.
Like there's already a lot going on.

I'm like, sorry, somebody squeezed past your robot.

It's our street.
It's our street.
For humans.
A government consultation into whether to bot or not is underway.
There's currently no national framework for the use of autonomous delivery robots.
But ministers are considering legalising their use across the UK.

Some charities are concerned about how this could impact vulnerable road users.

We think the people worst affected are those who are visually impaired, wheelchair users and then parents pushing prams,
particularly speaking about disabled people of which many of them tend to be elderly we actually know
that obstructed pavements lead to people making less walks journeys which can breed social isolation.
Starship Technologies the market leader in robot deliveries says it puts lived experience
of disability at the heart of what it does and works with disability charities to improve improved designs.
And the Department of Transport says whilst it welcomes innovation and advances in technology, it's vital the safety of pedestrians and vulnerable users is put first.
But with more than 10 million human -less deliveries already completed worldwide, has the robo -takeover
already begun?
Katie Roberts, BBC News.
The robo takeover.

and also James Barber the interim chief executive at the organisation Transport for All.

thank you what i feel like i'm tomorrow as well They'll play back these clips in years to come, remember when this was a novelty.
Lisa, I mean, there's an astonishing figure at the end of Katie's piece there, that there are already millions of these deliveries have already happened around the world.
Absolutely.
Starship have done more than nearly 2 million in the UK, 10 million globally.
So I know people think this is kind of the thing of the future, but actually for a bunch of communities, this is kind of just normal life now.
And that's when we know we've kind of winning is when it goes from being novelty and people going, oh, hey, there's a robot to it just been a part of normal Yeah,
because I live in Bristol and I've seen them on the streets now and again in Bristol.
That's one of the places where it's been trialled.
But where else have you been doing it in the UK?
That's right, so Bristol, they're not actually our robots in Bristol.
We are in Milton Keynes, Northamptonshire, West Yorkshire, just down the road actually in Greater Manchester in Sale,
Wakefield, a bunch of places across the UK where it is kind of just like normality now.
And what do people get delivered?
What do they use them for?
So the top delivery in the UK is actually milk.
So quite often it's top -up shopping.
So if you think about how deliveries changed and grocery shopping's changed over the last decade or so, people increasingly want small amounts of goods,
they want it quickly, and that demand has just exploded.
So the question is like, how do you meet that need that people have?
And so that's what the robots, that's the gap in the market really that the robots fill.
So quite often it's milk, bananas.
It's that top -up shopping when you've got the kids home from school, you've not got everything that you need for tea, so you get the robot to bring it for you.
So James they're filling a gap but they're also filling the pavements aren't they and for that reason you have some concerns?
Yeah so at Transport for All we are a national disability organisation representing the disabled community and one of our key issues is around the regulation
so at the moment there is a lack of regulation around delivery robots and as a result we have some real concerns around safety.
Our members and disabled people across the United Kingdom are saying that ultimately these are delivery robots
that are taking up large spaces on already congested pavements and we feel very closely
that disabled people and indeed everyone should own the pavements and people should always come before delivery robots and other potential barriers that could arise.
I mean is there a balance presumably in the same way that people who aren't maybe as capable of walking,
you know, actually having that as a way to deliver to them, that is helpful as well.
There is a balance there, is there?
Absolutely, and we fully accept that there are also some benefits to them.
Many disabled people will have different needs, so there are potential benefits that may arise from that, but for us those benefits don't always outweigh the harms.

When we have people with, say, sight impairments who are walking along long pavements which are already quite congested as it is they are raising serious concerns that these robots might not be able to pick up their supports their mobility aids and ultimately it can cause them quite serious injury if something goes wrong i think you have trained it lisa haven't you so that if the robot comes into conflict or or gets stuck with a mobility device it can send out a message i think we can get a demonstration of that now let's have a listen Hello, we seem to be in a tricky situation.
Don't worry, I've asked for a human to help remotely so you can be on your way as soon as possible.
It should only take a few seconds.
So how does that actually work when it's asking a human?
Is there a human tracking the movements of the robot at all times?
So the robot can call for help.
They're 99 % autonomous, so whenever you see them out in the wild, they're navigating on their own.
But in those rare occasions where they're like, actually, I don't know what to do here, or maybe I need a little bit of help, they'll just call a remote human who'll go, OK, I see what's happening.
I'm going to give you a little hand to help you out.
And so last year, for example, in the operating areas we've got in the UK, we identified around us around 23 ,000 mobility devices or wheelchairs.
There were very, very few incidents where we had to call someone to help the robot move or reverse because it's scanning the whole time.
So a bit like a person walking down a street, you can see if people are coming towards you, cars moving in the periphery.
The robot's got a 360 view of the world, so it kind of does have eyes in the back of its head and it can anticipate those problems before they happen.
But if we do get into that situation, the robot can call for help and resolve it as quickly as possible.
It's fascinating, isn't it, to think that this could become normal.
We will follow the debate and see how your campaign goes as well.
thank you very much indeed for coming in, all of you.
